sound_type: guttural croaking display -- low, guttural grunts and croaks at nest site; pig-grunt quality; individual display calls during courtship; smaller colony sizes than Brandt's; E=6
ecosystem: Rocky shore -- rocky shores, piers, bridges, jetties, pilings, buoys, and all types of human infrastructure adjacent to water; saltwater and freshwater; urban waterfront; Puget Sound year-round
season: Summer breeding (peak April-July); year-round Puget Sound resident; most active at breeding sites summer
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Recovered from DDT-era population suppression (egg-shell thinning). MMPA protected. Current conflict: Columbia River East Sand Island colony (up to 13,000 breeding pairs) predating ESA-listed juvenile salmon and steelhead. Army Corps of Engineers management: hazing, egg oiling, nest removal, lethal control. Same ESA/MMPA tension as California Sea Lion/steelhead at Ballard Locks (SPS 284).

coordinates.description: Year-round Puget Sound presence: Elliott Bay waterfront, Puget Sound pilings/buoys/piers/bridges, marinas throughout the Sound. Also freshwater lakes, rivers, reservoirs throughout Pacific Northwest. Columbia River estuary (East Sand Island colony, ESA conflict).

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Low, guttural grunts and croaks. Pig-grunt or frog-like quality. Produced in behavioral contexts: territorial defense, mate display (with orange-yellow gular pouch), chick recognition. Not musical; purely functional.
acoustic_data.colony_environment: Smaller and less dense than Brandt's; dozens to hundreds of breeding pairs typical. Orange-yellow gular pouch inflates during display calls. Less overwhelming colony acoustic mass than Brandt's thousand-pair colonies.
acoustic_data.e6_calibration: E=6: smaller colony sizes than Brandt's (E=7); lower individual call amplitude. More frequently encountered in urban Puget Sound context (year-round) but less acoustically imposing at any single colony site.

morphology.size: 28-35 inches (70-90 cm); wingspan 45-48 inches (114-123 cm); weight 2.5-4 lbs (1.1-1.8 kg)
morphology.appearance: Black with greenish iridescence; orange-yellow gular pouch; green eyes; hooked bill; double crest (tufts above eyes, breeding season only; black in Pacific Northwest subspecies albociliatum)
morphology.wing_spreading: Characteristic post-dive wing-spreading posture to dry partially-wettable plumage. Most common cormorant posture seen from Seattle waterfront on pilings, navigation markers, buoys.

nodes.title: Second and Final Phalacrocoracidae -- Chapter Closes
nodes.description: Degree 288 is the second Phalacrocoracidae entry following Brandt's (287). The chapter documents both PNW colonial cormorant strategies: outer coast specialist (287, Brandt's, E=7) and inland/coastal generalist (288, DCCO, E=6). A third species (Pelagic Cormorant) will enter the engine in future degrees.

nodes.title: The Puget Sound Cormorant -- Urban Waterfront Year-Round
nodes.description: Double-crested Cormorant is the cormorant of Puget Sound. Year-round resident. Encountered from Seattle waterfront, ferry decks, bridges. Wing-spreading posture on pilings, navigation markers, buoys, marina infrastructure. More frequently seen by Seattle residents than any other cormorant species. Brandt's is outer coast only.

nodes.title: Columbia River Salmon Conflict -- ESA vs. MMPA (2)
nodes.description: East Sand Island, Columbia River estuary: up to 13,000 DCCO breeding pairs predating ESA-listed juvenile salmon/steelhead smolts en route to ocean. Millions of juveniles consumed annually. Army Corps of Engineers management: hazing, egg oiling, nest removal, lethal control. Second major ESA/MMPA tension in PNW (first: Ballard Locks sea lions, SPS 284). Both: recovered protected species predating endangered species.

nodes.title: Habitat Generalism -- North America's Most Widespread Cormorant
nodes.description: Freshwater and saltwater; coastal and inland; urban and remote; Pacific coast to Atlantic coast. Trout, salmon (juveniles), herring, smelt, perch, sculpin -- whatever fish is available. The generalism explains the breadth: no dependence on any single prey type or habitat makes DCCO North America's most widespread cormorant.

cross_references.relationship: E=7. First Phalacrocoracidae. Outer coast specialist vs. inland generalist. Blue gular vs. orange-yellow. Thousands of pairs vs. dozens to hundreds. El Niño sensitive vs. resilient. Chapter comparison.
E=7. Urban waterfront haul-out. Both recovered MMPA species in ESA conflict: DCCO/salmon (Columbia River) and sea lion/steelhead (Ballard Locks). The recurring ESA/MMPA management challenge.
E=9. Third consecutive maximum. Post-hardcore, Shoreline. E=9 + E=6 = E=15. Both S36 and SPS blocks 285-288 close at degree 288.

milestones: THREE NEW SPS FAMILIES IN FOUR DEGREES (Phocidae, Hydrobatidae, Phalacrocoracidae)
